finding: Relative same-mode unlock-fee disadvantage is a robust platform pricing moderator.
evidence: unlock_fee_gap_to_same_mode_min_z x local_maas_only is negative in 6/6 models: all market FE = -0.089 (p < 0.001), all unit FE = -0.030 (p = 0.002). The multi-platform interaction is also negative in 6/6 models: all market FE = -0.091 (p < 0.001), all unit FE = -0.041 (p < 0.001).
interpretation: Platform membership makes relative access-price disadvantage especially costly: providers with higher unlock fees than same-mode competitors lose capacity utilisation particularly in Local MaaS and multi-platform contexts.
caveat: This is a relative price-position feature, not an exogenous price shock.

finding: Relative per-minute price disadvantage is weaker than relative unlock-fee disadvantage.
evidence: price_per_minute_gap_to_same_mode_min_z x local_maas_only is negative in 2/6 models; all market FE = -0.104 (p = 0.003), but all unit FE = -0.020 (p = 0.153).
interpretation: The platform pricing mechanism is more clearly about salient upfront access fees than about per-minute price gaps.
caveat: Use as supporting contrast, not as a main claim.

finding: Direct same-mode competition is only a moderate Local MaaS boundary condition.
evidence: same_mode_competitor_count_day_z x local_maas_only is negative in 2/6 models; all market FE = -0.059 (p = 0.013), all unit FE = -0.019 (p = 0.083). Any-platform and large-aggregator terms are not stable.
interpretation: Competition matters most when it creates direct same-mode alternatives in Local MaaS contexts, but competition alone is not the central mechanism.
caveat: Still weaker than the relative unlock-fee gap result.

finding: Own same-mode active-vehicle share generally helps platform contexts, but does not solve high-fee penalties.
evidence: active_vehicle_share_same_mode_z interactions are positive for large aggregator and multi-platform in 3/6 models; all unit FE = 0.027 (p = 0.008) for large aggregator and 0.019 (p = 0.024) for multi-platform. In high-fee models, unlock_fee_ge_1_00 x multi_platform x active_vehicle_share_same_mode_z is negative in 3/6 models; all unit FE = -0.074 (p = 0.008).
interpretation: Availability position can improve utilisation in platform contexts, but when the provider is also high-fee, higher availability does not offset pricing friction and may expose the mismatch more strongly.
caveat: Treat high-fee triple as mechanism probe; signs differ between market FE and unit FE.

finding: Local MaaS performs worse in night-oriented usage contexts.
evidence: night_trip_share_z x local_maas_only is negative in 4/6 models; all market FE = -0.019 (p = 0.001), all unit FE = -0.018 (p < 0.001).
interpretation: Platform effects are not only price-based: Local MaaS integrations appear less aligned with night/leisure-heavy usage patterns.
caveat: Process/context finding; secondary to pricing mechanism.

finding: Provider-level pass, free-unlock, reservation, and parking/geofence flags are useful coding assets but not robust estimators yet.
evidence: External flags have limited within-unit variation and generate rank/collinearity warnings in FE models; scooter-only pass/minute-bundle support has only one unique value.
interpretation: These features should guide richer external city-date coding and mechanism discussion, but should not be used as central estimated effects in the current panel.
caveat: Need historical city-date provider tariff/campaign variation to estimate causal moderation.

finding: Same-platform competitor count is currently not estimable cleanly.
evidence: same_platform_competitor_count_day interactions produce unstable, implausibly large coefficients and missing p-values in market FE, indicating collinearity with platform/city/operator structure.
interpretation: The idea is theoretically useful, but the current coding is too structurally tied to platform membership to estimate as a clean moderator.
caveat: Could be revisited with within-platform launch/exit timing or richer platform search-result data.
